Q1: I tried to keep a consistent routine, usually studying in the afternoons after my other activities. I'd block out time for hands-on practice since that's how I learn best - actually doing things rather than just reading. I aimed for about four hours a day spread across the week, though honestly some days I'd get really into it and study more, other days less. I liked to move around while studying, sometimes pacing or working at different spots around campus.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied more than I'd like to admit. I had good intentions about regular study times, but between my extracurriculars and other commitments, things shifted around a lot. Deadlines would make me buckle down more intensely, but during quieter weeks I'd sometimes let things slide. I think I studied a lot but not always at the right times or on the right things.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I needed to check assignment deadlines or download materials for offline use. Sometimes I'd get a notification about something new being posted and that would prompt me to check. I also logged in when I wanted to see if there was any feedback on my work or if discussion posts had new replies. It wasn't always a daily habit though - more like when I remembered or needed something specific.
Q4: For a while in the middle of the semester, I got into a rhythm of checking the LMS in the mornings with my coffee. It became almost automatic - wake up, check messages, check the course page. But that routine kind of fell apart when things got busier with my other activities. I never quite got back to that consistent pattern, which I think hurt me in the long run.
Q5: The video content was genuinely helpful because I could watch demonstrations and actually see how things worked. I spent a lot of time with those - over two hours worth I think. The downloadable practice materials were also great because I could work through them hands-on at my own pace. Anything interactive or practical felt more valuable to me than straight reading materials.
Q6: I mostly ignored the lengthy reading PDFs and some of the supplementary theory documents. They just didn't stick for me the way hands-on stuff did. The discussion forums I barely touched - I posted a few times but never really got into them. I kept telling myself I'd go back to those resources but never did, which probably wasn't smart.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could actually do something with it - apply it, practice it, see results. If I could physically work through a problem or simulation, I was all in. Activities that were just passive consumption felt like they were wasting my time even if they weren't. I needed to feel like I was building skills, not just absorbing information.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions rather than priorities, so I'd often skip them when time got tight. Required ones had deadlines attached, which gave them more urgency in my mind. Sometimes the optional stuff was actually more interesting, but without that external pressure I'd push it aside. I wish I'd treated some of the optional practice exercises as more important.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a ton of studying offline. I'd download the practice files and work through them on my laptop without being connected. I also used some YouTube tutorials and external resources that explained concepts in different ways. My notes from working through problems by hand were probably my most-used study materials, but none of that showed up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were whole weekends where I'd be working through downloaded exercises and my own notes, really grinding through the material, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd also study with classmates in person, talking through problems and explaining things to each other. Some of my best learning happened away from the computer entirely, just working things out on paper or whiteboard.
Q11: Yeah, that was basically my standard approach. I'd download everything in one session, then work through it over the next several days offline. The videos I'd sometimes watch once online then reference my notes instead of rewatching. It was more efficient for me to have everything locally so I could study wherever, like during commutes or between activities.
Q12: There was a rough patch mid-semester when I was feeling really down and sad about some personal stuff. My motivation was still there in theory, but actually sitting down to engage felt impossible some days. I kept up with assignments mostly, but my attendance dropped and I wasn't absorbing things as well. The mindfulness exercises they suggested helped a bit, but I was definitely struggling.
Q13: The workload from my extracurriculars definitely competed with course time. When I was tired from other activities, I'd log in and just kind of zone out looking at materials without really processing them. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those sections rather than tackle them head-on. I had the time on paper, but my mental energy wasn't always there.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit, honestly. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through content, but my mind was elsewhere. Sometimes I'd watch a video and realize at the end I hadn't retained anything. Those sessions felt productive in the moment but were basically wasted time. I think my sadness made it harder to really focus even when I was technically present.
Q15: I definitely postponed the more theoretical, reading-heavy sections because they didn't match how I learn. I knew they were important for the exams but I kept prioritizing the hands-on stuff I enjoyed more. The exam content I avoided probably explains why my exam score was so much lower than my assignment scores. It was easier to do what felt natural than push through discomfort.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my external motivation system. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very productive for a few days. Without them, I'd drift toward whatever interested me most. My assignment completion was decent because those had clear deadlines, but studying for exams - which had one big deadline - I kept putting off until too late.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it motivated me to keep that momentum going. But feedback often came back slowly, so by the time I got it I'd already moved on mentally. Positive comments made me feel like I was on the right track, which helped during the times I was feeling down. I wish there had been more immediate feedback loops.
Q18: The course structure gave me flexibility, which was good for fitting around my schedule but bad for my discipline. Having eleven online courses meant a lot of self-direction was needed, and I didn't always rise to that. The weekly modules helped create some rhythm, but I often fell behind and had to catch up in bursts. More structured checkpoints might have helped me stay on track.
Q19: The hands-on components and video demonstrations made engagement easier - those matched my learning style perfectly. What made it harder was the heavy reliance on reading materials and the relatively isolated nature of online learning. The discussion forums never felt engaging enough to pull me in. I also think the exam being weighted so heavily hurt me since my strength was in the practical assignments.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat - I was active in watching videos and downloading things, but my actual submitted work and exam performance didn't match that activity. Someone might be exploring, trying to understand, but struggling to translate that into completed work. Or they might be like me, engaging with the parts they enjoy while avoiding the parts that will actually be assessed.
Q21: That makes sense to me because so much real learning happens offline. Someone could download everything once, master it through their own methods, and ace the course without logging in much. The LMS is just one tool - it doesn't capture studying from notes, discussing with peers, or practicing independently. Low activity doesn't mean low effort or low learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns - when someone's active, what they click on, how long they spend. That's useful for spotting who's completely absent. But it misses the quality of engagement, whether someone's actually thinking or just scrolling. It can't see offline work, emotional state, or whether the time spent was actually productive. It's a partial picture at best.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, wanting to know more. For me it's physical too - being hands-on, actively working through problems. It's not just showing up or logging in; it's being mentally present and invested in learning. I was engaged with parts of this course, just not consistently or comprehensively.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled emotionally this semester, or how that affected my focus even when I was logged in. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with downloaded materials or practicing offline. It can't see that I'm a kinesthetic learner who needs to do things to understand them. And it definitely can't show that despite my poor exam score, I actually learned a lot of practical skills that I'll use going forward.
